summary: Gruber investigates whether iOS 13's autocorrect has regressed, prompted by his own experience of bizarre substitutions like 'Dobbs' being replaced by 'adobe'. After polling Twitter and receiving an overwhelming number of affirmative responses with equally baffling examples, he concludes this is a genuine regression rather than a nocebo effect. He analyzes the keyboard proximity logic that should govern autocorrect and finds iOS 13's substitutions violate it. He speculates that the new Slide to Type feature, enabled by default, may be the culprit and recommends disabling it as a potential fix.
key_points: iOS 13 autocorrect replaces correctly-typed dictionary words with contextually nonsensical alternatives, a behavior not seen in prior versions
The substitutions violate the fundamental keyboard-proximity heuristic that has governed autocorrect since the original iPhone
Overwhelming Twitter responses confirm this is a widespread regression, not individual perception bias
The new Slide to Type feature, enabled by default, is a plausible culprit for the degraded autocorrect behavior
The analysis of 'Dobbs' being replaced by 'adobe' demonstrates the substitution makes no sense by any keyboard proximity metric
money_quotes: When you type a dictionary word correctly — meaning you hit the exact right keys on the on-screen keyboard — iOS 13 autocorrect will replace it with a different dictionary word that makes no contextual sense.
I typed "Dobbs", including the Shift key for the "D", and iOS 13.1 autocorrected it to "adobe", with a lowercase "a".
Most famously, fuck gets replaced by duck because the D key is next to F on a QWERTY keyboard.
I tweeted this wondering if the autocorrect suckage I was seeing was just me — perhaps the result of a reverse placebo effect.
Now I'm close to convinced that, among iOS 13.0 and 13.1's numerous other bugs and problems, autocorrect has suffered a severe regression.
It's hard to overstate how much skepticism the iPhone faced over its lack of a hardware keyboard.
key_insight: iOS 13's autocorrect has suffered a genuine regression, replacing correctly-typed words with nonsensical alternatives that violate the fundamental keyboard-proximity logic that made the original iPhone keyboard trustworthy.
